title: Auto Tech Dispatch
lede: Stop playing phone tag with your technicians. Auto Tech Dispatch texts the right tech the moment a job comes in, escalates automatically when nobody answers, races your whole roster when speed matters, and even bridges masked calls between the tech and the customer — all without a dispatcher lifting a finger.

steps.title: Understand how auto-dispatch works
steps.content:
Auto Tech Dispatch is your 24/7 robotic dispatcher. The moment a job is created — from an inbound SMS, a booking form, a phone call, or manually — the engine picks the best technician and sends them a job offer by text. Here's the full lifecycle in plain language:

  • Score & pick — every active technician is scored on four factors (live GPS distance, home-zip proximity, priority rank, and current workload). The best score wins the first offer.
  • ACK or PASS — the tech replies with an acknowledgement keyword (like OK or K) to accept, or PASS to decline.
  • Escalate — if the tech doesn't reply within the ACK timeout, the engine texts the next-best tech, and the next, until someone accepts.
  • Confirm (optional) — with OTW Confirmation on, the tech must also tap On My Way in their tech dashboard before they're fully locked in.
  • Connect — after assignment, the system can automatically call the tech and bridge them to the customer through a masked number, so your customer never sees the tech's personal cell and vice versa.

There's also a second dispatch style — Broadcast / Race — that blasts the job to many techs at once, and the first to reply wins. You'll configure both below.

💡 Everything in this guide lives in one place: the Dispatch section of the app. No code, no external tools — just toggles and text templates.

steps.title: Open the Dispatch Dashboard
steps.content:
First, find your command center.

  1. In the top navigation bar, click Dispatch.
  2. A row of view chips appears under the header. Click Dispatch Dashboard (or go directly to /pipeline/jobs?tab=dispatch).
  3. You'll see live counters (Jobs, Draft, In Progress, Completed, Urgent, Available Techs), status tabs (All Jobs, Scheduled, Dispatched, On Hold, Cancelled…), and the jobs table itself.

The chip row is how you move around the whole Dispatch section: Statistics, Kanban View, Map, Messages, Team Members, Waiting ACK, Simulator, Trace — plus two action chips on the far right: Simulate SMS and Settings. Those last two are where the magic gets configured and tested.

images.alt: Dispatch Dashboard with counters, status tabs and the jobs table
images.caption: The Dispatch Dashboard — counters on top, status tabs, and every job in a live table.

images.alt: Dispatch Dashboard with counters, status tabs and the jobs table (dark theme)
images.caption: The Dispatch Dashboard — counters on top, status tabs, and every job in a live table.

steps.title: Open Dispatch Settings
steps.content:
All auto-dispatch behavior is configured in one modal.

  1. In the Dispatch chip row, click Settings (the gear chip at the far right — you may need to scroll the chip row).
  2. The Dispatch Settings modal opens. At the top, Send SMS From selects which of your phone numbers all dispatch texts are sent from — pick an SMS-enabled number here first.
  3. The left menu is grouped into: Job SMS (New Job, Job Update notifications), Inbound (SMS → Ticket, Status-Change SMS, Stale-Status, SMS Calendar Reminder), Auto Dispatch (Reply Handling, OTW Confirmation, Outbound Dispatch, Broadcast / Race, Calendar Sync, Link Calls to Jobs, Messages Templates), Call Masking (Tech Dashboard, Auto-Call, AI Post-Call Review), and Display (Dashboard Counters, Tabs, Kanban Columns).
  4. Each section shows an ON/OFF pill so you can see your whole automation posture at a glance. Click any section name to open it.
⚠️ Changes only apply after you click Save Changes at the bottom right. Click Close to discard.

images.alt: Dispatch Settings modal with grouped left navigation and ON/OFF pills
images.caption: The Dispatch Settings modal — every automation grouped in the left menu, with ON/OFF state pills.

images.alt: Dispatch Settings modal with grouped left navigation and ON/OFF pills (dark theme)
images.caption: The Dispatch Settings modal — every automation grouped in the left menu, with ON/OFF state pills.

steps.title: Turn on Outbound Dispatch and tune the selection algorithm
steps.content:
This is the master switch for sequential auto-dispatch — one tech at a time, best candidate first.

  1. In the left menu, under Auto Dispatch, click Outbound Dispatch.
  2. Flip on Auto-dispatch jobs to technicians. From now on, every newly-assignable job automatically picks the best candidate and texts them.
  3. Under Selection Algorithm, set the four scoring weights (lower combined score wins — set any factor to 0 to ignore it):
    • Live GPS — compares the tech's live phone location to the job address. Fresh GPS is the strongest signal; its influence fades as the location fix gets older.
    • Zip proximity — distance from the tech's home service ZIP to the job.
    • Rank — your manual priority order for techs (1 = first choice).
    • Load — how many open jobs the tech already has; busier techs score worse.
  4. Expand GPS freshness & capacity to control how old a GPS fix can be before it's ignored, and whether techs at their personal open-job cap are excluded entirely.
  5. Under Filters, use Excluded job sources and Excluded job types to keep certain work out of auto-dispatch (e.g. warranty callbacks you assign by hand).
💡 Per-technician limits — service radius, max open jobs, personal ACK timeout, working hours, and job type/source assignments — live on each technician's profile in the Team Members tab. A tech is only ever offered jobs that match their assigned job types and sources and fall inside their service radius.

images.alt: Outbound Dispatch settings with the master toggle and the four selection-algorithm weights
images.caption: Outbound Dispatch — the master toggle plus Live GPS / Zip proximity / Rank / Load weights.

images.alt: Outbound Dispatch settings with the master toggle and the four selection-algorithm weights (dark theme)
images.caption: Outbound Dispatch — the master toggle plus Live GPS / Zip proximity / Rank / Load weights.

steps.title: Set ACK keywords and escalation in Reply Handling
steps.content:
Reply Handling defines the conversation between the engine and your techs. This section is always active — it also serves manual and broadcast dispatches.

  1. In the left menu, click Reply Handling.
  2. Under Acknowledgement, edit ACK keywords — the words a tech can text back to accept a job. Defaults: OK, K, GOT IT, GOTIT, YES. (Techs reply PASS to decline and let the engine move on.)
  3. On ACK, set the job status to — optionally flip the job to a status like Dispatched the instant a tech accepts.
  4. Toggle Send a follow-up SMS after the tech acknowledges to auto-reply with the job number, address and any handoff details the moment they ACK.
  5. Under ACK Timeout, choose how long a tech gets to answer before the engine gives up on them and texts the next candidate. You can also send the unresponsive tech a courtesy "offer expired" message, and optionally notify the customer or referring partner when techs time out.
  6. Scroll further for Dashboard Issue Routing — what happens when a tech reports a problem from their dashboard (price issue, customer unreachable, can't do the job…). For each issue type pick Cancel, Escalate (pass the job to the next tech), Hold, or No Answer.
💡 The ACK timeout in this section is the tenant-wide default. Any individual technician can override it (or disable it) on their profile — handy for your most reliable techs.

images.alt: Reply Handling settings showing ACK keywords, on-ACK status and the ACK timeout section
images.caption: Reply Handling — ACK keywords, on-ACK job status, follow-up SMS and the ACK timeout.

images.alt: Reply Handling settings showing ACK keywords, on-ACK status and the ACK timeout section (dark theme)
images.caption: Reply Handling — ACK keywords, on-ACK job status, follow-up SMS and the ACK timeout.

steps.title: Add a second checkpoint with OTW Confirmation
steps.content:
An ACK proves the tech saw the text. OTW ("On The Way") Confirmation proves they're actually moving.

  1. In the left menu, click OTW Confirmation.
  2. Flip on Require an OTW tap after the ACK. Now an ACK alone doesn't lock the tech in — they must also tap On My Way in their tech dashboard.
  3. Under Wait Window, set How long to wait for the tech's OTW tap (in seconds) and Max reminders before escalating to the next tech.
  4. Under Messages, write the Reminder SMS to the tech that nudges them before the window expires, and choose what status the job takes when they confirm — or when they silently disappear and the job escalates to the next tech.

Leave this section off if an ACK reply should fully assign the job — the engine then stops at the ACK and the tech is committed immediately.

images.alt: OTW Confirmation settings with the wait window slider and reminder configuration
images.caption: OTW Confirmation — require an On My Way tap, with a wait window and reminders before escalation.

images.alt: OTW Confirmation settings with the wait window slider and reminder configuration (dark theme)
images.caption: OTW Confirmation — require an On My Way tap, with a wait window and reminders before escalation.

steps.title: Race your roster with Broadcast / Race mode
steps.content:
Sequential dispatch is polite. Broadcast is fast: text the job to many techs at once — the first to reply OK wins, everyone else gets a graceful "job taken" text.

  1. In the left menu, click Broadcast / Race.
  2. Flip on Allow broadcast / race dispatch.
  3. Request Message — the SMS sent to every tech in the race. Leave blank to reuse your standard Outbound Dispatch message (same {{tokens}} supported).
  4. "Job Taken" Message — what the losers receive. With Send it to choose Only techs who replied (default) or Every other tech.
  5. Race Timeout — decide what happens if nobody accepts in time: stop and mark the dispatch exhausted, fall back to a normal sequential run, or automatically re-broadcast to the techs who never answered (you control how many re-broadcast rounds).
  6. Scroll down for auto-start options: have every new job open as a broadcast automatically, choosing recipients by mode — all active techs, a fixed list, or only the techs the ranking algorithm considers eligible. Auto-start broadcast and sequential auto-dispatch are mutually exclusive — turning one on switches the other off.
  7. Optional extras: notify the customer when the race starts and when a winner is found, and give a second chance — if the winner later backs out, the job is automatically re-offered to the techs who had said OK the first time.

To fire a broadcast manually on a specific job, open the job, go to its Dispatch tab → Dispatch Controls → Broadcast / Race, pick the techs (or Select all active) and launch. A purple Broadcasting (race) tag with a live recipient list shows who replied what, in real time.

images.alt: Broadcast / Race settings with request message, job-taken message and race timeout
images.caption: Broadcast / Race — one blast, first ACK wins, and the rest get the "job taken" text.

images.alt: Broadcast / Race settings with request message, job-taken message and race timeout (dark theme)
images.caption: Broadcast / Race — one blast, first ACK wins, and the rest get the "job taken" text.

steps.title: Bridge tech and customer with masked auto-calls
steps.content:
Once a tech takes the job, the fastest way to lock in the appointment is a live call — so the platform can place it for you, through a masking number that keeps both sides' real numbers private.

  1. In the left menu, under Call Masking, click Auto-Call.
  2. Choose when the call fires:
    • When the tech self-assigns (SMS reply or dashboard claim) — the tech ACKed by text or claimed the job in their dashboard; the system immediately calls them and bridges them straight to the customer.
    • When an admin manually assigns from the job page — an operator picked the tech in the job detail view; optionally limit this to specific job statuses.
  3. Under AI Assistant, pick the Assistant that handles the outbound call — your AI agent dials the tech, briefs them, and connects the customer.
  4. Under Customer Caller ID, toggle Randomize the number the customer sees to rotate the masking number per job instead of always showing the same one.
  5. Scroll down for the retry loop: if the tech or customer doesn't pick up, the system re-tries the call on a schedule you define, and can set the job to a status of your choice when all attempts fail.

Also check the sibling Tech Dashboard section (masking for calls the tech starts from their dashboard) and AI Post-Call Review (an AI listens to the call recording and can update the job status with what it learned).

💡 Masked calling means your customer only ever sees your business number, and your tech never learns the customer's private number — no more off-platform side deals or misdials.

images.alt: Auto-Call on Assignment settings with trigger toggles, AI assistant picker and caller-ID randomization
images.caption: Auto-Call on Assignment — pick the trigger, the AI assistant that places the call, and the caller ID the customer sees.

images.alt: Auto-Call on Assignment settings with trigger toggles, AI assistant picker and caller-ID randomization (dark theme)
images.caption: Auto-Call on Assignment — pick the trigger, the AI assistant that places the call, and the caller ID the customer sees.

steps.title: Keep the customer in the loop automatically
steps.content:
Several toggles across the settings sections keep your customer (or the partner who sent you the job) informed without anyone typing a text:

  • On acceptance — when a tech ACKs a job that came in by SMS, the customer can get an instant auto-reply ("A technician has been assigned…"). Configure it in the SMS → Ticket section.
  • On timeout — in Reply Handling → ACK Timeout, send the customer a message when techs fail to respond, either after each tech times out or once when the whole dispatch is exhausted.
  • On broadcast start / win — in Broadcast / Race, message the customer when the race begins and when a winner is assigned.
  • On status change — the Status-Change SMS section (under Inbound) fires templated texts whenever a job hits a status you choose; Stale-Status nudges when a job sits too long in one state.
⚠️ If you enable both the on-ACK auto-reply and the broadcast "winner" message, a customer whose job was broadcast will get two texts when a tech accepts. Pick one per flow.

steps.title: Clean up stragglers in the Waiting ACK tab
steps.content:
Sometimes a dispatch never resolves — a tech with no timeout goes silent, or an old job is still technically "waiting". Those stragglers can hijack a tech's next "OK" reply, so the platform gives you a dedicated cleanup view.

  1. In the Dispatch chip row, click Waiting ACK.
  2. The Waiting for ACK table lists every job parked in a pending dispatch state, across all dates — with the job, its Dispatch State, the Technician currently being waited on, and Waiting Since.
  3. "Dispatched · no timeout" means that dispatch has no ACK timer, so it will never expire on its own.
  4. Click Clear on a row (or select several and clear in bulk) to end that dispatch's waiting state. Clearing only stops the dispatch state machine — it does not change the job's own status or assignment.
  5. Click Refresh to re-check after your techs reply.
💡 Make a habit of glancing at this tab. An empty list ("No jobs waiting for ACK 🎉") means every offer out there is live and accounted for.

images.alt: Waiting for ACK tab listing jobs parked in a pending dispatch state
images.caption: The Waiting ACK tab — every job still waiting on a technician acknowledgement, with one-click Clear.

images.alt: Waiting for ACK tab listing jobs parked in a pending dispatch state (dark theme)
images.caption: The Waiting ACK tab — every job still waiting on a technician acknowledgement, with one-click Clear.

steps.title: Dry-run everything with the Dispatch Flow Simulator
steps.content:
Before you point this machine at real customers, rehearse. The Flow Simulator runs your actual dispatch engine — real settings, real ranking, real escalation — against a specially flagged test job. No SMS is delivered and no calls are placed; every send is intercepted before it leaves the platform.

  1. In the Dispatch chip row, click Simulator.
  2. Click + New simulation (top right).
  3. In the New Dispatch Simulation modal:
    • Run mode — Step (manual) lets you advance the flow one event at a time; Auto-run (instant) plays the whole scenario through.
    • Inbound SMS → Ticket — the fake customer text that creates the job: sender number, SMS body (address, job type, notes), and optional job source/type overrides.
    • Technician behavior — script how your (real) techs respond in the simulation: default behavior like No answer (times out), a reply delay, and per-tech overrides (this one ACKs, that one PASSes…).
    • Auto-call outcomes — script whether each successive auto-call attempt connects or fails.
  4. Run it, then watch the run console: every SMS the engine would have sent, every escalation, timer and status change, in order.

It's the safest way to answer "what happens if my #1 tech ignores two jobs in a row?" — without texting anyone.

images.alt: Dispatch Flow Simulator tab with the safety notice and simulations list
images.caption: The Dispatch Flow Simulator — real engine, real settings, zero real texts or calls.

images.alt: Dispatch Flow Simulator tab with the safety notice and simulations list (dark theme)
images.caption: The Dispatch Flow Simulator — real engine, real settings, zero real texts or calls.

images.alt: New Dispatch Simulation modal with run mode, inbound SMS, technician behavior and auto-call outcomes
images.caption: New Dispatch Simulation — script the inbound SMS, how each tech responds, and the auto-call outcomes.

images.alt: New Dispatch Simulation modal with run mode, inbound SMS, technician behavior and auto-call outcomes (dark theme)
images.caption: New Dispatch Simulation — script the inbound SMS, how each tech responds, and the auto-call outcomes.

steps.title: Test the SMS → Ticket pipeline with Simulate SMS
steps.content:
The Simulate SMS action chip tests a different (and complementary) thing: the inbound side. It injects a message exactly as if a real customer had texted your number, so you can verify that the SMS → Ticket pipeline parses it, creates the job, and kicks off dispatch.

  1. In the Dispatch chip row, click Simulate SMS.
  2. In the Simulate Inbound SMS → Ticket modal, fill in:
    • Sender (From) — the fake customer's phone number.
    • Tenant DID (To) — which of your numbers "received" the text.
    • Message body — a realistic ticket text (job number, address, job type, customer number, notes). A sample is pre-filled.
    • Run through dispatch simulator — check this to route the resulting job into the Flow Simulator instead of real dispatch, so no real SMS or calls fire.
  3. Click Simulate Inbound SMS. Then watch the job appear on the Dispatch Dashboard — or in the Simulator tab if you checked the simulator option.
⚠️ Without the Run through dispatch simulator checkbox, this creates a real job — and if Outbound Dispatch is on, your techs will get a real dispatch text. Great for a final end-to-end test; just warn the team first. The modal also warns you if SMS → Ticket is disabled in settings, since no ticket would be created.

images.alt: Simulate Inbound SMS to Ticket modal with sender, message body and simulator options
images.caption: Simulate SMS — inject a customer text and watch it become a job, end to end.

images.alt: Simulate Inbound SMS to Ticket modal with sender, message body and simulator options (dark theme)
images.caption: Simulate SMS — inject a customer text and watch it become a job, end to end.

steps.title: Take manual control on any job
steps.content:
Automation with a steering wheel: every job's detail view has a Dispatch Controls card (on the job's dispatch/tech-history tab) where you can override the engine at any moment:

  • Pause / Resume — freeze the escalation clock without ending the run; the remaining time is restored on resume.
  • Restart — wipe the current run and dispatch from the top with fresh ranking.
  • Skip to tech — jump the queue and send the offer to a specific technician immediately (bypasses capacity and off-hours gates — a manual pick is intentional).
  • Manual picks — hand the engine an ordered list of techs to try, instead of the algorithm's ranking.
  • Broadcast / Race — launch a race for just this job (see the Broadcast step above).
  • Simulate (live) — a read-only dry-run that shows exactly how every tech scores for this job right now, including who was excluded and why (out of radius, at capacity, off hours…), with weight sliders to play what-if. Nothing is sent.

The same view shows the live countdown to the next escalation, each tech's response history, and a per-run debug panel — so you always know what the robot is doing and why.

💡 Wondering why a specific tech didn't get the job? Open the job's Simulate (live) panel — every exclusion reason is spelled out per technician.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference
  • 🎥 Watch our video tutorials

metaDescription: Complete guide to Auto Tech Dispatch in iMash: SMS job offers to technicians, ACK/PASS replies, automatic escalation, broadcast/race mode, OTW confirmation, masked auto-calls between tech and customer, customer notifications, the Waiting ACK cleanup tab and the risk-free dispatch simulators.
metaKeywords: auto dispatch, technician dispatch, SMS dispatch, broadcast race dispatch, ACK PASS, OTW confirmation, call masking, dispatch simulator, field service automation, iMash
